item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I wonder if SRA works on anchoring one or some -2 longer to get interviewed?
The concept is good, though I don't really get the whole virtualization thing, perhaps ELI5 me?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Downvoted. On the surface, it's another "weird place that wasn't there before", except without a hefty hook.
With the had-read-the-comments-to-comprehend hook, it's another "our universe is in someone else's backyard" without a hefty hook.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm afraid this is currently a negative vote for me. I work in IT and didn't get what was going on here. The fact that the facility was in some way "software" was fine, but the whole virtualization of reality angle never raised a periscope out of the subtext.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Noting that on July 19th, I reverted an edit by user Alton Blake that fell outside the allowable range of corrections for spelling, grammar and/or punctuation errors.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm not sure it's obvious enough what's going on here, but then again, if it was too obvious it'd be weird for the Foundation not to comment on it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't think i like this particular skip, neutral vote so far. If this particular foundation is a virtual one, and that justifies the keter class then it should be reflected somewhere in the article, not under a spoiler in the comment section, amairite?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I get where you're coming from, and there's definitely something to be said for bluntness and brevity, but personally I favor the cryptic and arcane articles. A light breezy article that just hands the idea to you is good for a momentary chill or gee whiz!, but the ones that always stick with me are the convoluted ones that take you on a trip down the rabbit hole or that you have to think about for a while before the "Wait a second…o_o;" really hits you.

Its sort of like eating shelled sunflower seeds or shrimp; having to work at a little before you can get at the good part makes it taste all the better, and is actually part of the appeal.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm afraid this doesn't appeal to me.

I respect the high concept, but in general, articles that only make sense because of an on-the-nose line in a final interview seem a little played out to me — I prefer interviews that add to a concept that stands on its own, and this doesn't. Why are the entities superpowered?

which had previously been [DATA EXPUNGED] due to its memetic effects

There's a lot of redactions like this that don't add anything. I would focus on making the writing of higher-concept articles like this breezier and more accessible.

-1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I have to say I really enjoy something like this. It's not spooky or creepy or anything but the implications of it could be horrific if you just sat and thought about it for a while.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This reminds of a recent article that was more "the building is alive", but I think it stands apart, and generally I like it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Reading tgjer's and your posts, I finally get it. That makes sense.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think it's meant like:

"I can't be assed to do this right now."

Basically another word for bothered.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is the kind of SCP that I like. You don't *have* to put everything out there for the reader to discover, there's room for imagination to work. I like it, except for one…tiny…detail:

Subject-1: Thirty years lost because they couldn't be assed to do 5-year backups. Like I said, we can't be giving them our people. What year do they have to start from? Like, 1984?

"assed"? Shouldn't it be "asked"? Or is it an attempt to portray an ethnic voice? Not sure.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's Keter because it's not contained at all.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think it's Keter because the implication is that the Foundation's universe is a virtual system, existing alongside other virtual systems inside a computer or something analogous to a computer. The entities working within 2244 are users who exist outside that computer and can manipulate it, and presumably could even delete the Foundation's universe from the outside if they wanted to.

The Foundation doesn't know who these users are, what they're doing, who they work for or what their motivations are. They may have created the Foundation's universe entirely.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not sure I follow why this is a Keter level.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Liked it in the forums, liked it here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why is this Keter in the first place?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So, if I'm understanding you and Wikipedia correctly,

+Such spoil
-So reveal

the Foundation universe is software, and parallel universes are other sets of software. The building is a file moved from one virtual computer to another.

Close or no cigar?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
not even worth clicking on
– hide block

Unlike simulations, virtual machines are as fully functional as physical machines, and capable of interacting and connecting with other devices just the same.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+Spoilers?
– hide block

The Foundation universe is a computer simulation?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
spoilers do not read pointless info here
– hide block

Virtualization is the emulation of a computer, operating system, or application on a physical system. Using Virtualization, you can create dozens of different systems or machines on one physical computer. Together, they all share the same CPU, land, memory, and network interfaces. Yet each world functions independently, with its own logically defined resources, systems, and interfaces.

Meanwhile to a user, a virtual machine appears and acts no differently from a physical machine running the same software.
